content:
# Acts Chapter 18 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), PDF pages 247-248 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/ACT_018_page_001.jpg, ACT_018_page_002.jpg

---

**1** After these things, having departed from Athens, he came to Corinth. **2** And finding a certain Jew named Aquila, a native of Pontus by birth, having recently come from Italy, and Priscilla his wife — because Claudius had commanded all the Jews to depart from Rome — he came to them, **3** and because he was of the same trade, he stayed with them and worked, for they were tentmakers by trade. **4** And he was reasoning in the synagogue every Sabbath, and was persuading both Jews and Greeks.

**5** And when both Silas and Timothy came down from Macedonia, Paul was occupied with the word, testifying to the Jews that Jesus is the Christ. **6** But when they opposed and blasphemed, he shook out his garments and said to them, "Your blood be upon your own head; I am clean. From now on I will go to the Gentiles." **7** And having departed from there, he went into the house of a certain man named Titius Justus, a worshiper of God, whose house was adjoining the synagogue. **8** And Crispus, the ruler of the synagogue, believed in the Lord with his whole household; and many of the Corinthians, hearing, were believing and being baptized.

**9** And the Lord said to Paul in the night through a vision, "Do not be afraid, but speak, and do not be silent, **10** because I am with you, and no one will attack you to harm you, because there are many people belonging to me in this city." **11** And he stayed a year and six months, teaching the word of God among them.

**12** But when Gallio was proconsul of Achaia, the Jews with one accord rose up against Paul and brought him before the judgment seat, **13** saying, "This man is persuading people to worship God contrary to the law." **14** But when Paul was about to open his mouth, Gallio said to the Jews, "If indeed it were some wrongdoing or wicked crime, O Jews, I would with reason bear with you; **15** but if these are questions about a word and names and the law among you, see to it yourselves. I do not wish to be a judge of these things." **16** And he drove them away from the judgment seat. **17** And they all seized Sosthenes, the ruler of the synagogue, and beat him before the judgment seat. And none of these things were of concern to Gallio.

**18** And Paul, having remained yet many days, took leave of the brothers and sailed away to Syria, and with him Priscilla and Aquila, having shorn his head in Cenchreae, for he had a vow. **19** And they came to Ephesus, and he left them there. And he himself entered into the synagogue and reasoned with the Jews. **20** And when they asked him to remain for a longer time, he did not consent, **21** but took leave of them, saying, "I will return to you again, God willing." And he set sail from Ephesus.

**22** And having come down to Caesarea, he went up and greeted the church, and went down to Antioch. **23** And having spent some time there, he departed, passing through the region of Galatia and Phrygia in order, strengthening all the disciples.

**24** Now a certain Jew named Apollos, an Alexandrian by birth, an eloquent man, arrived at Ephesus, being powerful in the Scriptures. **25** This man had been instructed in the way of the Lord, and being fervent in spirit, he was speaking and teaching accurately the things concerning Jesus, though knowing only the baptism of John. **26** And this man began to speak boldly in the synagogue. But when Priscilla and Aquila heard him, they took him aside and explained to him the way of God more accurately.

**27** And when he wished to cross over into Achaia, the brothers encouraged him and wrote to the disciples to welcome him. And having arrived, he greatly helped those who had believed through grace, **28** for he was powerfully refuting the Jews publicly, showing through the Scriptures that Jesus is the Christ.

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

### Textual Observations from Codex Sinaiticus

1. **Verse 2 — "Priscilla" (PRISCILLA):** Sinaiticus consistently uses the form Priscilla (Priskilla) rather than the diminutive "Prisca" found in some Pauline epistles. The formal translation preserves this form throughout.

2. **Verse 5 — "occupied with the word" (synecheto to logo):** Sinaiticus reads synecheto, "was constrained/pressed" by the word, indicating Paul's intensified focus on proclamation upon the arrival of his co-workers from Macedonia.

3. **Verse 7 — "Titius Justus":** Sinaiticus reads the name as Titiou Ioustou. Some manuscripts read "Titus Justus" or simply "Justus." The Sinaiticus reading is preserved here.

4. **Verse 12 — "Gallio" (GALLIWNOS):** Lucius Junius Gallio Annaeanus, proconsul of Achaia ca. 51-52 AD. This is one of the most important chronological anchors in the New Testament, confirmed by the Delphi inscription.

5. **Verse 17 — "they all seized Sosthenes":** Sinaiticus reads "pantes" (all). The identity of "all" — whether Greeks or Jews — is ambiguous. Some have identified this Sosthenes with the one Paul later names as co-sender of 1 Corinthians (1 Cor 1:1), suggesting a subsequent conversion.

6. **Verse 18 — "having shorn his head in Cenchreae":** The grammatical antecedent of the participle keiramenos is ambiguous — it could refer to Paul or to Aquila. The proximate grammar most naturally favors Paul as the subject, and this is the traditional reading preserved here. The vow is likely a Nazirite vow (cf. Numbers 6:1-21).

7. **Verse 21 — Shorter reading in Sinaiticus:** Sinaiticus preserves a shorter text at verse 21, omitting the phrase "I must by all means keep the coming feast in Jerusalem" found in some later manuscripts. This shorter reading is consistent with the Alexandrian text-type.

8. **Verse 25 — "the way of the Lord":** Sinaiticus reads "ten hodon tou kyriou" (the way of the Lord). Some manuscripts read "the way of God." The Sinaiticus reading is preserved.

9. **Verse 26 — "Priscilla and Aquila":** Sinaiticus places Priscilla before Aquila, a sequence also found in several other early manuscripts. This word order may reflect Priscilla's prominence in the teaching ministry of this couple.

10. **Verse 27 — "through grace":** The phrase "dia tes charitos" can be construed either with "those who had believed" (they believed through grace) or with "helped" (he helped through grace). The formal translation preserves the ambiguity by placing it at the end of the clause, as in the Greek.

### Translation Philosophy

This chapter was translated following formal equivalence principles, preserving Greek word order and syntactic structure where English permits. Participial constructions are retained rather than converted to finite verbs. Theological terms — Christ (Christos), Lord (Kyrios), grace (charis), the word (ho logos) — are rendered consistently throughout.

— **LOGOS**
